subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Timbs v. Indiana (2019)
stimulus:
In November of 2013, Tyson Timbs was arrested after selling illegal drugs to an undercover police officer. Timbs pleaded guilty to the charges and was sentenced to a year of house arrest, five years probation, and a $1,200 fine.

In addition, the state sought to seize Timbs's Land Rover, which Timbs used to transport the illegal drugs. The Land Rover was previously purchased for $42,000. Timbs argued that the vehicle seizure constituted an excessive fine, which was a violation of the Eighth Amendment. The maximum fine for Timbs's crime was $10,000, which was considerably less than the price Timbs paid for the Land Rover.

In the resulting case, Timbs v. Indiana (2019), the Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of Timbs. The opinion of the Court, authored by Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg, stated that the excessive fines clause is a provision that applies to the states. The Court found that the protection against excessive fines is "fundamental to our scheme of ordered liberty" and "deeply rooted in this Nation's history and tradition."

parts.prompt: Identify the legal doctrine that is common in both Timbs v. Indiana (2019) and McDonald v. Chicago (2010).
parts.scoring_notes: Only selective incorporation (or incorporation doctrine) earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the legal doctrine that is common in both Timbs v. Indiana (2019) and McDonald v. Chicago (2010).

required_elements: The legal doctrine common in both cases is selective incorporation.
alternatives.correct_example: The legal doctrine common in both cases is selective incorporation.

parts.prompt: Explain how the holding in McDonald was similar to the holding in Timbs.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate information about McDonald. Second point requires connecting both cases through the doctrine of selective incorporation — both applied Bill of Rights provisions to the states.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the holding in McDonald was similar to the holding in Timbs. (1 point — describe relevant fact/holding about the required Supreme Court case)

required_elements: In McDonald, the court held that the Second Amendment applied to states.
alternatives.correct_example: In McDonald, the court held that the Second Amendment applied to states.

required_elements: McDonald incorporated the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms for the purpose of self-defense to the states.
alternatives.correct_example: McDonald incorporated the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms for the purpose of self-defense to the states.

common_traps: Describing only one case without explaining the comparison does not earn the 2-point tier

scoring_points.description: Explain how the holding in McDonald was similar to the holding in Timbs. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different/similar holdings)

required_elements: In McDonald, the court held that the Second Amendment applied to the states. In Timbs, the court held that the excessive fines clause of the Eighth Amendment applied to the states. Both cases involved the incorporation of civil liberties from the Bill of Rights to the states.
alternatives.correct_example: In McDonald, the court held that the Second Amendment applied to the states. In Timbs, the court held that the excessive fines clause of the Eighth Amendment applied to the states. Both cases involved the incorporation of civil liberties from the Bill of Rights to the states.

common_traps: Describing only one case earns 1 point, not 2
Must connect facts to different outcomes

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Timbs affects the reserve powers of the states.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the specific limitation on state power created by the Timbs decision — states' sentencing and asset forfeiture powers are now constrained by the Eighth Amendment's excessive fines clause.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the decision in Timbs affects the reserve powers of the states.

required_elements: The decision in Timbs incorporated the Eighth Amendment to the states and will limit states’ powers related to sentencing and punishments. States will not be able to pass laws that provide for punishments exceeding the constitutional protections against excessive fines. Total for question 3
alternatives.correct_example: The decision in Timbs incorporated the Eighth Amendment to the states and will limit states’ powers related to sentencing and punishments. States will not be able to pass laws that provide for punishments exceeding the constitutional protections against excessive fines. Total for question 3
